      How= long-term dietary cholesterol can slow down its own clearance by liver cel= ls

      =20 =20

      Cholesterol carried by low-density lipoprote= in (LDL) drives heart disease and is cleared by liver cells expressing the = receptor LDLR. A cell-signalling mechanism has been discovered through whic= h high cholesterol promotes the enzyme-mediated degradation of LDLR. Blocki= ng this enzyme restores LDLR levels in liver cells, suggesting a new strate= gy for treating high cholesterol.

      =20 =20 =20 =20 =20
      =20
      =20 Research Briefing25 Jun 2026 =20 =20

      Ele= ctric fields probe the symmetry of the =E2=80=98heavy hydrogen=E2=80=99 nuc= leus

      =20 =20

      The response of the nucleus of a deuterium a= tom to electric fields shows no evidence of asymmetry, which is consistent = with conventional theories of particle physics.

    Die= tary cholesterol activates a Ral-dependent pathway driving LDLR turnover

    =20 =20

    Chronic dietary cholesterol activates Ral GT= Pases, which promote LDLR internalization and lysosomal degradation through= RalBP1=E2=80=93REPS1 and CTSA, thereby reducing cholesterol clearance, whe= reas CTSA inhibition restores LDLR function and may offer a therapeutic str= ategy for cardiovascular disease.
